2006_On the Stability of Fine-tuning BERT - Misconceptions
column4: # On the Stability of Fine-tuning BERT: Misconceptions

title: 1. Debunk popular explanations: `Catastrophic forgetting and small data size are not the reason for fine-tuning instability.`
- Catastrophic forgetting is the byproduct of optimization failure.
- Dataset Size is NOT the root cause of BERT fine‑tuning instability
content: but the reduced total training steps is.

title: 2. `Fine-tuning instability results from optimization failure` with 2 core issues with empirical evidence.
- `Vanishing gradients` (optimization failures) in early-training (causing most fail runs):
Solution: `Adam with bias correction` / `learning rate warm up` / `gradient clipping` / ...
- `Generalization Varience` (low training loss doesn’t guarantee high performance on validation data) in late-training (causing performance variance in success runs):
Solution: `Train longer` (more iterations/epochs) / `pick or average over multiple random seed runs` / ...
1. Optimizer: Use `Adam with bias correction enabled`
content: countering vanishing gradients at the start.

title: 2. Learning Rate & Schedule: Keep lr small (~2×10⁻⁵)
3. Longer Training: Instead of the usual 3 epochs

title: 1. To increase adaptivity
content: set multiple adapter modules during training.

title: 2. To not add compute or tunable parameters during training
content: only activate one adapter module each layer through random routing.

title: 3. To not add compute or tunable parameters during inference
content: equally merge separately for "project-down" and "project-up" matrices in each layer.

title: 4. To regularize model uncertainty from random routing
content: add symmetric KL div into the loss function

column8: `identify dead neurons` in SAE which have not fired in any of the previous 12

title: > Resampling too frequently causes training to diverge.
2. `Compute the loss` for the current model on a random subset of 819
3. Assign each input vector a `probability` of being picked that is proportional to the `square of the autoencoder’s loss` on that input.
> Higher loss data has higher chance to be picked in the following steps.
4. For each dead neuron `sample an input` according to these probabilities.
Give the dead neuron a new `dictionay vector` by setting it as the `renormalized input vector` (unit L2 norm).
> Force dead neurons to represent high loss data.
5. For the corresponding `encoder vector`
